0a-what-is-package-json.theory: The package.json file is a manifest file used to describe the metadata of a Node.js project, including its dependencies, scripts, and other settings. It's a crucial file for managing project dependencies and running scripts. npm (Node Package Manager) is a package manager for Node.js that allows users to easily install and manage dependencies.
0a-what-is-package-json.connection: In our Real-Time Collaborative Text Editor project, we'll use package.json to declare our project's dependencies, including the WebRTC library we'll use for real-time communication. We'll also use npm to install and manage these dependencies.
0a-what-is-package-json.syntax_explanation: In package.json, we'll use the 'dependencies' field to specify our project's dependencies, and the 'scripts' field to define custom scripts for our project. A script is just a key-value pair where the value is the terminal command to run.
0a-what-is-package-json.challenge: Add the WebRTC library as a dependency in your package.json file and define a 'start' script to run 'node server.js'.

0b-commonjs-vs-es-modules.theory: Node.js historically used CommonJS (require/module.exports) but now supports modern ES Modules (import/export). ESM is the standard for modern JavaScript and is what we will use to organize our code into reusable pieces.
0b-commonjs-vs-es-modules.connection: In our Collaborative Editor, we need a way to generate unique IDs for every user that connects. We will write a utility function in ESM syntax and export it so our server can use it.
0b-commonjs-vs-es-modules.syntax_explanation: Use the 'export' keyword before a function or variable to make it available to other files.
0b-commonjs-vs-es-modules.challenge: In utils.js, create and export a function named 'generateId' that returns a random string. You can use the code example provided.

0c-running-scripts.theory: To run a Node.js file, you use the 'node' command followed by the filename in your terminal (e.g. 'node server.js'). This executes the JavaScript file within the V8 engine.
0c-running-scripts.connection: Our server.js will be the entry point for our real-time editor backend. Let's make sure we can import our utility function and run the file.
0c-running-scripts.syntax_explanation: Use the 'import' keyword to bring in functions from other files. Remember to include the '.js' extension when importing local files in Node.js ESM.
0c-running-scripts.code_example:
import { generateId } from './utils.js';

console.log('Server starting... Generated ID:', generateId());
0c-running-scripts.challenge: In server.js, import 'generateId' from './utils.js' and console.log it. Note: you must replace the previous boilerplate code entirely.
0c-running-scripts.golden_snippet:
import { generateId } from './utils.js';

console.log('Server starting... Generated ID:', generateId());

0d-basic-http-server.theory: Node.js provides a built-in 'http' module to create web servers. An HTTP server listens for incoming network requests on a specific port and sends back responses.
0d-basic-http-server.connection: Our collaborative editor needs a server to serve the frontend files and handle WebSocket connections. We will start by creating a basic HTTP server.
0d-basic-http-server.syntax_explanation: Import 'createServer' from 'http', pass it a callback function handling (req, res), and call .listen(port) on the returned server object.
0d-basic-http-server.challenge: In server.js, replace your previous code to import 'createServer' from 'http' and start a server on port 3000. Send back 'Hello World' to any request.

1-what-is-node-js-really.theory: Node.js allows JavaScript to run outside the browser using the V8 engine and libuv.
1-what-is-node-js-really.connection: We need a data structure in memory to represent the state of our text editor before we can send it over the network.
1-what-is-node-js-really.syntax_explanation: Use class syntax to define a class, and constructor() to initialize its properties.
1-what-is-node-js-really.challenge: Create and export a 'CollaborativeDocument' class with a constructor that initializes 'this.content = ""' and 'this.clients = new Set()'.

2-the-event-loop-explained.theory: The event loop processes asynchronous callbacks (like timers) while continuing to execute synchronous code.
2-the-event-loop-explained.connection: To ensure our server is running smoothly, we'll add a periodic background task to log the server's health.
2-the-event-loop-explained.syntax_explanation: setInterval takes a callback function and a delay in milliseconds, executing the callback repeatedly.
2-the-event-loop-explained.challenge: Add a setInterval that logs 'Server health check...' to the console every 10,000 milliseconds.

3-process-nexttick.theory: process.nextTick defers execution to the very beginning of the next event loop iteration, prioritizing it over other async tasks.
3-process-nexttick.connection: We want to log a startup message immediately after the server begins listening, but guarantee that the synchronous execution finishes first.
3-process-nexttick.syntax_explanation: process.nextTick takes a callback that will be invoked immediately after the current operation completes.
3-process-nexttick.challenge: Inside your server.listen callback, use process.nextTick() to log 'Document system initialized'.

4-memory-management-garbage-collection.theory: V8 manages memory automatically, but retaining references to objects you no longer need will cause memory leaks.
4-memory-management-garbage-collection.connection: When users disconnect from our editor, we must explicitly remove them from the clients Set to avoid leaking memory.
4-memory-management-garbage-collection.syntax_explanation: Use the .add(value) and .delete(value) methods on a Set instance to modify its contents.
4-memory-management-garbage-collection.challenge: Add addClient(id) and removeClient(id) methods to CollaborativeDocument. removeClient must call this.clients.delete(id).

5-the-process-object.theory: The process object provides information about the current Node.js environment, including environment variables via process.env.
5-the-process-object.connection: Hardcoding port 3000 is bad practice for deployment. We should allow the host environment to specify the port.
5-the-process-object.syntax_explanation: Access environment variables on the process.env object, and use the logical OR (||) operator for fallbacks.
5-the-process-object.challenge: Update your .listen call to use process.env.PORT || 3000 instead of a hardcoded 3000.

6-asynchronous-patterns.theory: Modern JavaScript uses async/await and Promises to handle non-blocking code cleanly compared to nested callbacks.
6-asynchronous-patterns.connection: We will eventually save our document state to a database or file. Let's start by scaffolding an asynchronous save method.
6-asynchronous-patterns.syntax_explanation: Use the 'async' keyword before a method name, and return a new Promise.
6-asynchronous-patterns.challenge: Add an 'async save()' method to CollaborativeDocument that returns a Promise resolving to true after a setTimeout of 500ms.

7-the-path-module.theory: The path module provides utilities for working with file and directory paths across different operating systems.
7-the-path-module.connection: We need to determine exactly where to save our document files on disk safely.
7-the-path-module.syntax_explanation: Use path.join() to concatenate strings into a valid file path for the current OS.
7-the-path-module.code_example:
import path from 'path';
const fullPath = path.join(process.cwd(), 'logs', 'error.log');
7-the-path-module.challenge: Import the 'path' module. Export a function 'getDocumentPath(filename)' that uses path.join to combine process.cwd(), 'data', and the filename.

8-fs-readfilesync-vs-fs-readfile.theory: Synchronous I/O blocks the event loop, while asynchronous I/O does not. Synchronous is mostly used for startup configuration.
8-fs-readfilesync-vs-fs-readfile.connection: When our server starts, it should synchronously attempt to load a startup config file before accepting connections.
8-fs-readfilesync-vs-fs-readfile.syntax_explanation: readFileSync(path, 'utf8') returns the file contents synchronously. Wrap it in a try-catch to handle errors if the file is missing.
8-fs-readfilesync-vs-fs-readfile.challenge: Import readFileSync from 'fs'. Above your server definition, write a try-catch block that attempts to read 'config.json' (use 'utf8'), logging 'No config found' if it fails.

9-the-fs-promises-api.theory: The fs/promises API allows you to perform file system operations using async/await without blocking the event loop.
9-the-fs-promises-api.connection: Let's replace our mock save method with actual file I/O to persist the collaborative text to disk.
9-the-fs-promises-api.syntax_explanation: Import functions like writeFile from 'fs/promises', and use 'await' to wait for their completion.
9-the-fs-promises-api.challenge: Import writeFile from 'fs/promises'. Update your save(filename) method (remove setTimeout) to 'await writeFile(filename, this.content, "utf8")'.

10-watching-files-and-directories-for-ch.theory: fs.watch allows your application to listen to operating system events regarding file modifications.
10-watching-files-and-directories-for-ch.connection: If our config.json changes on disk while the server is running, we can log a warning without needing to restart the server.
10-watching-files-and-directories-for-ch.syntax_explanation: watch(path, callback) executes the callback with an event type ('rename', 'change') and the filename when something changes.
10-watching-files-and-directories-for-ch.challenge: Import watch from 'fs'. Call watch('.') and provide a callback (eventType, filename) => { console.log('File changed:', filename); }.
